figures.gloss: Demigod who snares the sun to slow the day, fishes whole islands from the sea, and steals fire for people.
figures.facet: The trickster here is a demigod-hero, not a departmental atua: his transgressions belong to the mortal/hero register (a human mother, a final death) even as his feats reorder sun, fire, and land — kept consistent with hawaiian-maui's own kind, and with the fact that he literally steals fire from Mahuika, the culture-hero act proper.
figures.provenance: Grey, Polynesian Mythology (1855); Tregear, The Maori-Polynesian Comparative Dictionary; White, The Ancient History of the Maori.

figures.gloss: Son of Rangi and Papa, atua of the ocean and the father of fish and reptiles. When the storm-brother Tāwhirimātea attacks the children who parted their parents, Tangaroa flees into the deep; his descendants split, the fish staying in the sea and the lizards taking refuge inland — a primal quarrel that, Māori say, set sea against land forever.
figures.facet: The deep here is not a slain chaos but an aggrieved BROTHER who retreats: the sea-god withdraws into his own element under the storm's assault, and the rift between his ocean-children and his land-children is read as the standing enmity of water and shore.
figures.provenance: Grey, 'Polynesian Mythology' (the children of Rangi and Papa); Best. Across wider Polynesia (Tahiti, Hawai‘i as Kanaloa) Tangaroa is elevated far higher — in Māoridom he is one brother among the storm-scattered six.

figures.gloss: The lightning hero of the East Polynesian cycle: son of Hemā, he avenges his father's blinding and murder by the sea-dwelling Ponaturi, then climbs a vine to the heavens to find his ancestors and win esoteric knowledge (karakia) for humankind; the flash of lightning is said to come from his eyes, or from the opening and shutting of his armpits.
figures.facet: Ascent, not descent, is the route to sacred knowledge here: unlike heroes who steal fire or secrets from a jealous power below, Tāwhaki must physically climb a vine that collapses under a crooked approach — making right conduct itself the precondition for reaching heaven.
figures.provenance: Grey, Polynesian Mythology (1855); Tregear, The Maori-Polynesian Comparative Dictionary (1891); White, The Ancient History of the Maori (1887-90); the cycle recurs across Māori, Cook Islands, Tuamotuan and Hawaiian (as Kaha'i) tradition.

figures.gloss: The navigator credited in Māori tradition with the first sighting and naming of Aotearoa, having pursued the giant octopus Te Wheke-a-Muturangi across the Pacific from Hawaiki in his canoe Matawhaorua; his return report of the new land is said to have opened the way for the later migration canoes, and coastal place-names are attributed to his voyage.
figures.facet: Discovery is remembered as a named, individual act of pursuit rather than an anonymous drift: the land is found because a specific man was chasing a specific monster, tying the whole country's arrival story to a single hunt rather than a generalized migration.
figures.provenance: Grey, Polynesian Mythology (1855); S. Percy Smith's Kupe reconstructions (contested); oral traditions of various iwi recorded by 19th- and 20th-c. ethnographers — details and chronology vary significantly by source lineage.

figures.gloss: The last, unborn or still-infant child of Ranginui and Papatūānuku, left inside his mother's body (or at her breast) when his brothers tore their parents apart and turned Papatūānuku face-down; his movements in the dark below are earthquakes and volcanic activity, and some traditions credit him with governing the turning of the seasons and the fires that warm his mother from within.
figures.facet: Geological violence is domesticated into a family arrangement: earthquakes are not a monster's rage but a child stirring against his mother's body, sequestered below because the primal separation of sky and earth left him no room to be born.
figures.provenance: Grey, Polynesian Mythology; Best, Maori Religion and Mythology; the figure belongs to the same Ranginui/Papatūānuku/Tāwhirimātea cycle held in the corpus's separate 'maori' tradition file.

figures.gloss: The Tahitian god of war and fertility, ascendant at the great marae of Taputapuātea on Ra'iātea by the 18th century, patron of the Arioi society whose traveling performers propagated his cult across the Society Islands; his rise subordinated older Tahitian deities in the generations before European contact, and his priesthood and rites were recorded by early missionaries and voyagers.
figures.facet: A pantheon is caught mid-reshuffle: 'Oro's cult is a documented 18th-century religious innovation rather than a timeless fixture, showing Polynesian religion actively reordering its own hierarchy of gods within living historical memory, not only in myth-time.
figures.provenance: William Ellis, Polynesian Researches (1829); J. A. Moerenhout, Voyages aux îles du Grand Océan (1837); Douglas Oliver, Ancient Tahitian Society (1974), on the Arioi and 'Oro's cult at Taputapuātea.

figures.gloss: In many Māori and wider East Polynesian traditions, the first man — in some tellings shaped from earth by Tāne alongside or instead of Hine-ahu-one, in others a separately self-generating first being; the carved ancestor/fertility image and the hei-tiki neck pendant take his name and are passed down as treasured heirlooms.
figures.facet: The tradition holds two incompatible anthropogonies side by side without resolving them: humanity's origin is Tāne shaping and animating a woman of earth in one account, and Tiki as a separately created first man in another — variance kept rather than smoothed over.
figures.provenance: Grey, Polynesian Mythology; Tregear, The Maori-Polynesian Comparative Dictionary; the hei-tiki pendant tradition is well documented in Māori material culture (e.g., Te Papa Tongarewa collections).

figures.gloss: The supreme sky-being of Samoan cosmogony: existing alone in the void, he made the heavens and sea from a rock, sent the bird Tuli to find resting land for his children, and in some accounts formed the first humans; distinct Tagaloa figures multiply through the genealogies, and by the 19th century his active cult was reported as thin compared to the vigor of the myth cycle itself.
figures.facet: The same root that means 'sea/ocean' god elsewhere in Polynesia (Tangaroa, Kanaloa, Ta'aroa) is promoted in Samoa to supreme sky-creator — one linguistic root doing entirely different theological work depending on which island group inherited it.
figures.provenance: George Turner, Samoa: A Hundred Years Ago and Long Before (1884); John Fraser's 19th-c. Samoan mythology collections; Craig, Handbook of Polynesian Mythology (1989).

figures.gloss: The land of the dead and of the gods in Tongan and Samoan tradition, generally located to the west and sometimes identified with Fiji or with the western point of Savai'i; the spirits of the dead, and in Tongan tradition the supernatural ancestors of the Tu'i Tonga line, are said to journey there.
figures.facet: West Polynesia keeps origin and afterlife as two separable geographies where East Polynesian tradition tends to fold them into one remembered homeland: Pulotu is where the dead go, not also — as with Hawaiki — where the ancestors are said to have sailed from.
figures.provenance: E. W. Gifford, Tongan Society (1929); Craig, Dictionary of Polynesian Mythology (1989); George Turner on Samoan Pulotu traditions.

edges.note: Both are trickster culture-heroes who wrest boons from the gods by guile; Maui fishes up islands, Anansi buys the stories.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Apex divine powers of the ocean, governing the treacherous domain of seafarers and marine predators.
Both are primordial wonder-working culture-heroes who reshape the world by cunning and craft — Väinämöinen by song, Māui by trickery and the magic hook; structural kin, no contact.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A legendary culture hero whose specific actions irrevocably cement mortality for all future generations.

Kanaloa < Proto-Polynesian *Taŋaroa by exact reflex — *t > k, *ŋ > n, *r > l — the same ocean-god as Māori Tangaroa / Samoan Tagaloa; cognate of the deep, with the Hawaiian peculiarity that he is Kāne's companion rather than a paramount sky-source.

One and the same hero by descent: Proto-Polynesian *Maaui, the demigod who fishes up islands, snares the sun, and steals fire, shared across Polynesia with extraordinary fidelity — the Hawaiian and the wider-Polynesian Māui are the identical figure.

Two trickster culture-heroes who teach humankind, fight monsters, and reshape the world by audacious cunning — the Khoekhoe ancestor and the Polynesian demigod both die in the end, but Heitsi-Eibib keeps rising where Maui is finally killed.
Culture-hero tricksters whose gifts to humankind come wrapped in mischief and overreach — Iktómi the namer, Māui the fire-snarer and sun-snarer; benefaction inseparable from the prank.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The quintessential Oceanic trickster-hero who battles his brothers and wrests vital elements (like night/day) from ancient powers for humanity's benefit.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are culturally beloved, semi-divine trickster-heroes who rely on cunning rather than raw power to steal boons for humanity and alter the geography.
Both are trickster culture-heroes who master the sun — Maui snares and slows it, Raven steals and releases it. Parallel of the trickster bending the solar order to human use.

The storm drives Tangaroa into the deep; in the flight his sea-children and land-children split, setting ocean against shore — the Māori etiology of why the lizard fled inland and the fish stayed in the sea.

Why death stands: Māui crawls into the sleeping Hine-nui-te-pō to win immortality for humankind, but she wakes and crushes him in the obsidian teeth between her thighs — the trickster's one defeat, and the reason death has never been undone.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Rapa Nui theology merged the ancient Polynesian sea god Tangaroa into the new, localized supreme figure of Makemake.
Both are trickster culture-heroes who reshape the cosmos by audacious acts and who give humankind its conditions of life; |Kaggen makes the moon as Maui hauls up lands and snares the sun.

The Māui cycle is written into descent and landscape alike: iwi traditions recite lines from Māui himself, and the North Island is Te Ika-a-Māui, the fish he hauled up, the South Island his canoe — the trickster fixed as ancestor and geography.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: sovereign powers of the sea governing fish and the fisherman's luck, propitiated before every catch — independently arrived at across Uralic and Polynesian waters.

Same Rangi/Papa sibling-set myth, split across the corpus's adjacent 'polynesian' and 'maori' tradition files — Rūaumoko is the youngest brother, left inside their mother when the others tore the parents apart.
The unborn son remains at his mother Papatūānuku's breast; his stirring below is read as her own inner fire and tremor.
Two incompatible Māori/East-Polynesian anthropogonies for the first human — Tāne shaping Hine-ahu-one from earth in one telling, Tiki as first man in another; the variance is the datum.
Tagaloa < Proto-Polynesian *Taŋaroa, the same reflex chain already noted for Hawaiian Kanaloa — but Samoan theology promotes the name to supreme sky-creator rather than keeping it sea-god alone.

Both are the far geography orienting the cosmos — Hawaiki the remembered homeland of origin and return in Māori tradition, Pulotu the land of the dead in Tongan/Samoan tradition — kept distinct since they do different work in their island groups.

The two great demigod-hero cycles of East Polynesian oral literature, often recited in sequence — one ascending to heaven by right conduct, the other descending by trickery — complementary hero-types rather than the same figure.

Wandering trickster-culture-heroes who return transformed with a boon for their people — Guahayona bringing back guanín and cibas, Māui snaring the sun and fishing up islands; a loose structural rhyme, flagged as a reach given how different the specific feats are.

dossier.essence: The traditional religions of the Polynesian peoples of the central and eastern Pacific — Māori, Hawaiian, Tahitian, Samoan, Tongan, and others — descended from a common Ancestral Polynesian religion that formed in the Tonga-Samoa homeland (c. 1000-500 BCE) and diversified as the islands were settled. This dossier holds the shared substrate rather than any one island's telling of it, catalogued here through the two figures who carry it under recognizably the same name and role wherever Polynesians settled: Māui, the trickster-demigod who fishes up islands, snares the sun, and steals fire, and Tangaroa, the sea-god descended by regular sound-change from Proto-Polynesian *Tagaloa. The fuller, elaborated pantheon and cosmogony this substrate supports is catalogued at its living, best-documented fullest in the Māori tradition (maori).
dossier.cosmos: Comparative myth and historical linguistics recover a common inheritance beneath the island-by-island variation: a shared trickster-demigod cycle and a sea-god whose name descends by regular sound-law from Tongan/Samoan Tagaloa through Tahitian Ta'aroa and Hawaiian Kanaloa to Māori Tangaroa, alongside the twin regulating concepts of mana (efficacious power) and tapu (restriction) attested from Hawaii to Aotearoa to Tahiti. Each society then elaborates this shared material into its own cosmogony and its own ranking of the gods — Tangaroa/Kanaloa, for one, stands paramount or near-paramount in Tahiti and prominent in Hawaii, but is one junior brother among six in the Māori telling.
dossier.practice: Across the islands, life is regulated by tapu (sacred restriction) and by the winning and guarding of mana; ritual is conducted by trained priestly experts — tohunga in Māori, kahuna in Hawaiian — through chanted invocation and offering. The open ceremonial court (marae in Māori and Tahitian usage, its Hawaiian cognate the heiau) is a shared architectural form for communal ritual across the region, elaborated differently by each island group.
dossier.canon: Oral throughout: genealogies and cosmogonic or mythic chant held in trained memory, first set down in writing during the 19th century by missionary and ethnographic collectors working island by island.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds a shared regulatory logic — mana and tapu — and a common cast of gods and heroes carried, under recognizably the same names, across the Polynesian triangle; the cosmos-as-genealogy elaboration this substrate supports is catalogued at its fullest in the Māori tradition (maori), not narrated again here.
sources: Patrick V. Kirch & Roger C. Green, 'Hawaiki, Ancestral Polynesia: An Essay in Historical Anthropology' (2001)
